**Shanghai Shenhua Football Club** ([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 上海申花足球俱乐部; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Shànghǎi Shēnhuā Zúqiú Jùlèbù*) is a Chinese professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), that competes in the [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League"), the top tier of [Chinese football](/wiki/Chinese_football_league_system "Chinese football league system"). Shanghai Shenhua plays its home matches at the [Shanghai Stadium](/wiki/Shanghai_Stadium "Shanghai Stadium"), located within [Xuhui District](/wiki/Xuhui,_Shanghai "Xuhui, Shanghai").

The owner of Shanghai Shenhua is [Shanghai Jiushi Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiushi_Group "Shanghai Jiushi Group"),[[3]](#cite_note-Shanghai_Jiushi_Group-3) a state-owned cultural and sports operation company in Shanghai. Shanghai Shenhua is one of the four clubs to have never been relegated from the Chinese top-flight since the [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League")'s foundation in [2004](/wiki/2004_Chinese_Super_League "2004 Chinese Super League"). The term *shen hua* literally translates as "the Flower of Shanghai" in English – *shen* is one of the alternative names for Shanghai and *hua* means flower in Chinese.

The club's predecessor was the municipal-run semi-pro club **Shanghai Football Club**. The team predominantly played in the top tier, where they won several domestic league and cup titles. On 10 December 1993, the club was reorganized to become a completely professional football club so they could play in the [1994 Chinese Jia-A League](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1994 "Chinese Jia-A League 1994") season, making them one of the founding members of the first fully professional top-tier leagues in China. Shenhua have won 6 [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup") and a record 5 [Chinese FA Super Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Super_Cup "Chinese FA Super Cup").

According to *Forbes*, Shenhua was the 6th most valuable football team in China, with a team value of $106 million, and an estimated revenue of $29 million in 2015.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early club")]

Shanghai Shenhua's predecessor was originally called **East China**, a team name used as far back as 1910 for football in the multi-sport event [Chinese National Games](/wiki/National_Games_of_China "National Games of China").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The local [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") government sports body decided to use this name for their new club, founded on 1 November 1951, to take part in China's first fully nationalized national football league tournament, where they finished second in the league that year.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The football league gradually expanded and the team was allowed to name themselves after their own province of Shanghai in 1957. Soon afterwards, by 1961, Shanghai started to establish themselves as a major football team within China when they won their first league title.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This was then quickly followed by their second league title in 1962. However, in 1966, because of the Chinese [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), football in China was halted and Shanghai was unable to play. When football returned in China, Shanghai was able to return to the top tier. However, they were unable to regain any of the dominance that they had previously shown and were even relegated in 1980.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Though they were able to be quickly promoted in the following season, they spent many years without actually winning any titles until [Wang Houjun](/wiki/Wang_Houjun "Wang Houjun") led them to win the [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup") in 1991, which was their first trophy in 29 years.[[9]](#cite_note-data.sports.163.com-9)

### Professionalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Professionalism")]

Throughout the 1990s, the [Chinese Football Association](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association "Chinese Football Association") was demanding more professionalism from their football teams and while many were semi-professional, Shanghai would be one of the first when they gathered sponsorship from Yu Zhifei and the local company named Shenhua ("Flower of Shanghai") on 10 December 1993, founding Shanghai Shenhua.[[9]](#cite_note-data.sports.163.com-9)
This then saw Shanghai hire their first professional manager in [Xu Genbao](/wiki/Xu_Genbao "Xu Genbao"), who was the previous [China national team](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") manager, in 1994. The move would quickly see Shanghai win the second professional football league title by the end of the 1995 league season.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
When Xu left, Shanghai attempted to bring in several foreign coaches to add more experience to the team. However, few achieved any success despite being close on several occasions, except for [Muricy Ramalho](/wiki/Muricy_Ramalho "Muricy Ramalho")'s brief spell when the club won the 1998 Chinese FA Cup.
By the end of 2001, the Shenhua group ended their sponsorship of the club and were replaced by SVA and the [Shanghai Media & Entertainment Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Media_%26_Entertainment_Group "Shanghai Media & Entertainment Group"). The club changed its name to Shanghai Shenhua SVA SMEG Football Club. The team, however, remained unique as it still retained "Shenhua" in its name, whereas many other teams dropped the name of their former sponsors completely.
On the pitch, the club would take over [Shanghai 02](/wiki/Shanghai_02 "Shanghai 02"), a youth football team set up by Xu Genbao, while also bringing in a new manager in [Wu Jingui](/wiki/Wu_Jingui "Wu Jingui"), who built a new squad predominantly using many from the Shanghai 02 squad and despite struggling in his debut season, he was able to win the league title in 2003.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Zhu Jun era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Zhu Jun era")]

In 2007, the owner of inner-city rival [Shanghai United](/wiki/Shanghai_United_F.C. "Shanghai United F.C."), [Zhu Jun](/wiki/Zhu_Jun_(businessman) "Zhu Jun (businessman)") and his company [The9 Limited](/wiki/The9_Limited "The9 Limited"), bought a majority share of Shanghai Shenhua and began to merge Shanghai United into Shanghai Shenhua. His first act was to replace the previously successful existing head coach [Wu Jingui](/wiki/Wu_Jingui "Wu Jingui") with Shanghai United's [Osvaldo Giménez](/wiki/Osvaldo_Gim%C3%A9nez "Osvaldo Giménez").[[12]](#cite_note-12) The appointment was to prove highly disruptive and Wu Jingui was quickly brought back as the head coach after only a few months, but was sacked on 9 September 2008. [Jia Xiuquan](/wiki/Jia_Xiuquan "Jia Xiuquan") took over his position on the same day.[[13]](#cite_note-13) This was followed by the club adding to their backroom staff when, on 1 January 2009, Shenhua made Chinese football history by becoming the first Chinese team to hire a foreign CEO and a technical director. The club hired former manager Osvaldo Gimenez as their chief executive officer.[[14]](#cite_note-14) One day later, former [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") technical director [Stan Valckx](/wiki/Stan_Valckx "Stan Valckx") joined Shenhua in the same position.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Drogba_and_Anelka.jpg/250px-Drogba_and_Anelka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drogba_and_Anelka.jpg)

[Didier Drogba](/wiki/Didier_Drogba "Didier Drogba"), [Giovanni Moreno](/wiki/Giovanni_Moreno "Giovanni Moreno"), and [Nicolas Anelka](/wiki/Nicolas_Anelka "Nicolas Anelka") against [Guangzhou Evergrande](/wiki/Guangzhou_Evergrande "Guangzhou Evergrande") in July 2012.

After a disappointing 2011 season in the [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League"), Zhu Jun decided to bring in a marquee player, so on 12 December 2011, it was confirmed that [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") striker [Nicolas Anelka](/wiki/Nicolas_Anelka "Nicolas Anelka") would be arriving in Shanghai in January 2012, while six days later, it was announced that his compatriot [Jean Tigana](/wiki/Jean_Tigana "Jean Tigana") would be the head coach from the 2012 season. Tigana was fired after a string of poor results and was replaced by former [Argentina national team](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") coach [Sergio Batista](/wiki/Sergio_Batista "Sergio Batista") to lead the team. After a successful season playing for [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") and winning the [2011–12 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_UEFA_Champions_League "2011–12 UEFA Champions League"), [Ivorian](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_national_football_team "Ivory Coast national football team") striker [Didier Drogba](/wiki/Didier_Drogba "Didier Drogba") signed a two-and-a-half-year deal with Shenhua.[[16]](#cite_note-16) This was soon followed by the signing of [Colombian](/wiki/Colombia_national_football_team "Colombia national football team") international footballer [Giovanni Moreno](/wiki/Giovanni_Moreno "Giovanni Moreno") from Argentinian club [Racing Club](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Avellaneda "Racing Club de Avellaneda"). These signings were intended to boost the club's title challenge and see Zhu Jun's investment within the club reach 150 million [Yuan](/wiki/Chinese_yuan "Chinese yuan"), which he believed gave him a controlling stake of 70 percent, as promised by the other shareholders. When the other shareholders decided not to agree to this arrangement, Zhu Jun decided to pull his funding of the club, which resulted in the team finishing in a disappointing ninth place and both Anelka and Drogba leaving the club.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The relationship between Zhu Jun and the other shareholders became even more fractious at the beginning of the 2013 league season when the Chinese FA issued the club with a six-point deduction for match-fixing ten years prior and a fine of one million Yuan. This would lead to a shareholder dispute between the other shareholders SVA, Shanghai Media Group, Shanghai Electric Group, and Huangpu SASAC on who should pay for this fine, which saw a gap in the club finances that saw [Rolando Schiavi](/wiki/Rolando_Schiavi "Rolando Schiavi"), [Patricio Toranzo](/wiki/Patricio_Toranzo "Patricio Toranzo"), and Giovanni Moreno refuse to play the 31 March 2013, league game against [Liaoning Whowin](/wiki/Liaoning_Whowin_F.C. "Liaoning Whowin F.C.") because of unpaid wages.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Controversy")]

Critics would dispute the legitimacy of the title win after it was discovered in 2011 that the referee [Lu Jun](/wiki/Lu_Jun_(referee) "Lu Jun (referee)") was [bribed](/wiki/2003%E2%80%932009_Chinese_football_match-fixing_scandals "2003–2009 Chinese football match-fixing scandals") by the head of the CFA's referee arrangements, Zhang Jianqiang, to be biased towards Shenhua in a vital match against [Shanghai COSCO Sanlin](/wiki/Shanghai_COSCO_Sanlin "Shanghai COSCO Sanlin") in a game that Shenhua won 4–1.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Lu Jun and Zhang Jianqiang were both officially charged with match-fixing, while Shenhua's general manager Lou Shifang was discovered to be the person who orchestrated the bribes. Initially, despite this indiscretion, the club was spared any disciplinary action.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The reason provided by the CFA at the time for the leniency was that they would be punishing the individuals who put the game in disrepute and not the club; because Lou Shifang was Shenhua's offending participant and had left the club several years before the allegations were confirmed, it would have been harsh to punish the club retrospectively.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

On 18 February 2013, The CFA would decide to change its mind on Shenhua and retrospectively decided to punish the club by [revoking its 2003 league title](/wiki/2003%E2%80%932009_Chinese_football_match-fixing_scandals "2003–2009 Chinese football match-fixing scandals"), fining the club with 1 million Yuan and giving a 6-point deduction at the beginning of the [2013 Chinese Super League](/wiki/2013_Chinese_Super_League "2013 Chinese Super League") season after it was discovered that they also fixed another game against [Shaanxi National Power](/wiki/Shaanxi_National_Power "Shaanxi National Power") en route to winning the 2003 league title.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-english.cri.cn-23)

### Greenland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Greenland")]

The Zhu Jun era ended on 31 January 2014, when the club was purchased by [Greenland Holding](/wiki/Greenland_Holdings "Greenland Holdings") Group Company Limited who bought a share of 28.5% of the club.[[24]](#cite_note-绿地宣布接手申花_朱骏时代宣告终结-24) On 6 February 2014, Greenland Holding Group Company Limited announced that the club's official name would be changed to "Shanghai Greenland FC, Shanghai Greenland Shenhua team" and it was hoped that by retaining Shenhua within the official team name, it would appease the fans by reflecting on the club's heritage.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) This did not work. Subsequent badge alterations which eliminated Shenhua from the team's logo drew significant criticism from many of the club's supporters, who publicly voiced their dissatisfaction on 9 March 2014, during the league game against [Shanghai Shenxin](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenxin_F.C. "Shanghai Shenxin F.C.") as they saw removing Shenhua from the club's name as a stain on the team's heritage and history.[[27]](#cite_note-27) On 18 July 2014, the club bowed to pressure from their supporters when they officially released a new team badge, which brought Shenhua back into the team logo and subsequently changed the club's name to "Shanghai Greenland Shenhua Football Club".[[28]](#cite_note-28)

On 3 February 2015, three days after the [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") won the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup"), [Tim Cahill](/wiki/Tim_Cahill "Tim Cahill") announced he had been signed by the Shenhua, moving from the [New York Red Bulls](/wiki/New_York_Red_Bulls "New York Red Bulls")[[29]](#cite_note-29) Despite signing a one-year contract extension in November 2015, [Tim Cahill](/wiki/Tim_Cahill "Tim Cahill") announced on his [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") on 16 February 2016, that his contract had been terminated by incoming coach [Gregorio Manzano](/wiki/Gregorio_Manzano "Gregorio Manzano").[[30]](#cite_note-30) No reason was given for his termination beyond saying that he was "not part of the new coach Manzano's plans for the 2016 season..."[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Shanghai Shenhua won the [2019 Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/2019_Chinese_FA_Cup "2019 Chinese FA Cup"), beating [Shandong Luneng Taishan](/wiki/Shandong_Taishan_F.C. "Shandong Taishan F.C.") 3–0 at a packed [Hongkou Stadium](/wiki/Hongkou_Football_Stadium "Hongkou Football Stadium") on 6 December 2019, making it a 3–1 aggregate victory for the Blues. It was the fifth time Shenhua had lifted the trophy, and the second time in three years, after their [2017 Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/2017_Chinese_FA_Cup "2017 Chinese FA Cup") victory over city rival [Shanghai SIPG](/wiki/Shanghai_SIPG_F.C. "Shanghai SIPG F.C.").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In 2021, the CFA launched its "neutral name campaign", requiring professional clubs to switch to names that do not mention their sponsors. The club, though, always had been publicly recognized as "Shenhua", had different sponsor prefixes and suffixes (such as "Greenland Shenhua" and "Shenhua SVA") in the last thirty years. Responding to the call for neutral names, the club owner, Greenland, restored the name of the club to Shanghai Shenhua without prefixes or suffixes on 9 February 2021.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

On 23 November 2022, the club was deducted six points for unpaid salaries.[[34]](#cite_note-HEBSHSWHY-34) The club would admit that they had been in financial difficulties for over a year and it was exacerbated by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). However, they have resolved the issue and were looking for additional financial investment.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Jiushi Era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Jiushi Era")]

On 6 January 2023, the club declared that the [Shanghai Jiushi Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiushi_Group "Shanghai Jiushi Group") would become the owners of the club.[[3]](#cite_note-Shanghai_Jiushi_Group-3)

In 2026, Shanghai Shenhua started the [2026 Chinese Super League season](/wiki/2026_Chinese_Super_League "2026 Chinese Super League") with 10 points deducted for violation of sports ethics and loss of sportsmanship, engaging in improper transactions to seek illegitimate benefits.[[36]](#cite_note-match-fixing2-36)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C.&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main articles: [Shanghai derbies](/wiki/Shanghai_derbies "Shanghai derbies") and [Jing–Hu rivalry](/wiki/Jing%E2%80%93Hu_rivalry "Jing–Hu rivalry")

Shenhua's fiercest and oldest rivalry is against [Beijing Guoan](/wiki/Beijing_Guoan_F.C. "Beijing Guoan F.C."), and the rivalry is often referred to as the [China Derby](/wiki/Jing%E2%80%93Hu_rivalry "Jing–Hu rivalry").[[37]](#cite_note-37) The rivalry with Beijing is viewed as a manifestation of the rivalry that exists between the two most important cities in the country, as one is the center of government while the other is the [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") of modern commerce within China.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Each club had an extensive history, including successful periods. However, they rarely competed directly for trophies until the [1997 league season](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1997 "Chinese Jia-A League 1997"). With Shenhua having won the 1995 league title and Beijing having won the 1996 Chinese FA Cup, both teams looked as if they had the pedigree to win trophies that season and on 20 July 1997, in a vital league game, [Beijing thrashed Shenhua 9–1](/wiki/Beijing_Guoan_9%E2%80%931_Shanghai_Shenhua "Beijing Guoan 9–1 Shanghai Shenhua") at the [Workers Stadium](/wiki/Workers_Stadium "Workers Stadium") in Beijing.[[39]](#cite_note-39) It would be Beijing's largest victory and Shenhua's greatest defeat ever recorded. Soon after that event, both teams would meet again in the 1997 FA Cup final, which saw Beijing win the cup.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

When professionalism was established in [1994](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1994 "Chinese Jia-A League 1994") within the Chinese leagues, it opened the door for more than one team within each city. This eventually paved the way for the first-ever Chinese top-flight city derby, which took place in 2002 when Shanghai Shenhua lost 2–0 to [Shanghai Zhongyuan](/wiki/Shanghai_Zhongyuan "Shanghai Zhongyuan") (later renamed Inter) in front of a sold out Hongkou Football Stadium. Known as the Shanghai derby, it would be the start of an intense but short rivalry between the two clubs, which reached its peak on the final day of the 2003 league season with both teams within reach of winning the league title.[[41]](#cite_note-shanghai-derby-41) Shenhua won their game while Inter surprisingly lost theirs to relegation fighting club [Tianjin Kangshifu](/wiki/Tianjin_Kangshifu "Tianjin Kangshifu"). This saw critics dispute the title win and it was eventually discovered that both teams had players and officials match-fix games throughout the campaign.[[23]](#cite_note-english.cri.cn-23) Shenhua would retrospectively lose their title while the Inter owners decided it was financially unviable to remain in Shanghai and relocated their team to [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), which effectively ended the rivalry.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

With Inter Shanghai leaving the city, Shenhua experienced another one of these Shanghai derbies when [Shanghai United](/wiki/Shanghai_United "Shanghai United") was promoted in the [2006 league season](/wiki/2006_Chinese_Super_League "2006 Chinese Super League"). The rivalry between the two teams never reached the same intensity as what was experienced against Inter because United had only recently relocated to the city and was building their fan base.[[41]](#cite_note-shanghai-derby-41) Any development of a rivalry was ultimately cut short when Zhu Jun took over both teams and merged them together, with Shenhua keeping their name. In 2012, [Shanghai Shenxin](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenxin_F.C. "Shanghai Shenxin F.C.") moved to the city, revitalizing the derby. However, it was the promotion of [Shanghai SIPG](/wiki/Shanghai_SIPG_F.C. "Shanghai SIPG F.C.") in 2013 that caught the fans' imagination because they were formed by [Xu Genbao](/wiki/Xu_Genbao "Xu Genbao"), who had previously managed Shenhua.[[41]](#cite_note-shanghai-derby-41) The club's geographical location has also opened them up to rivalries with neighbouring clubs [Hangzhou Greentown](/wiki/Hangzhou_Greentown_F.C. "Hangzhou Greentown F.C.") and [Jiangsu Suning](/wiki/Jiangsu_Suning_F.C. "Jiangsu Suning F.C."), where they contest in a fixture called the Yangtze Delta Derby.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Later, with the relegation of Shanghai Shenxin in 2015 and then disbandment in 2020, Shanghai SIPG became the sole rival for Shenhua in Shanghai. The competition reached its peak when Shenhua beat SIPG in the [2017 Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/2017_Chinese_FA_Cup "2017 Chinese FA Cup") finals on aggregate and when SIPG won the [super league](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League") soon after, in 2018. The rivalry between the fans and the players made the new Shanghai derby arguably the most exciting derby in China. Also, in 2020, with Jiangsu Suning lifting the domestic league, Jiangsu fans escalated the cross-town rivalry by renting a billboard showcasing Jiangsu's trophy just outside the Hongkou Stadium, Shenhua's home venue. This act of provocation incited massive rebuke from the Shenhua fans, resulting in the ad being removed the day after. However, though fierce the rivalry might have been through the years, it concluded with Jiangsu Suning's dissolution in 2021.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
